worth_it_explanation: Almost everything LinkedIn measures on a website runs through the Insight Tag, so a gap here removes website conversions, retargeting audiences, and website demographic reporting at the same time. The check is quick. Campaign Manager shows the tag status and the list of domains it has seen traffic on, and a tag inspector confirms the tag fires on the pages that matter with the right Partner ID. The fix is a placement or settings change, not a rebuild, and the data starts flowing again from the moment it is corrected.
intro: The LinkedIn Insight Tag is a lightweight piece of JavaScript that sits on every page of a website and reports member visits back to LinkedIn. It is the foundation for website conversion tracking, website retargeting audiences, and the website demographic reporting that shows which job titles, companies, and industries visit the site. These checks confirm that the tag is present and firing across the whole site, that exactly one copy fires per page with the correct Partner ID, and that every domain receiving campaign traffic is recorded in the tag's settings.

audit_checks.title: The LinkedIn Insight Tag is not installed or is not firing across the site
audit_checks.impact_rationale: The Insight Tag is the source of LinkedIn's website measurement. When it is absent, or firing on only some pages, website conversions go uncounted, retargeting audiences cannot build, and website demographic reporting has no data to draw from. Campaigns optimizing toward conversions are then working from an undercount or from nothing at all.

educate.base:
The LinkedIn Insight Tag is a small block of JavaScript that LinkedIn provides in Campaign Manager. It carries a Partner ID that ties the tag to the ad account, and it reports each member visit back to LinkedIn so the account can count website conversions, build website retargeting audiences, and read website demographic reporting.

For the tag to cover the whole site, it has to load on every page. LinkedIn recommends placing it in the site's global footer, just before the closing body tag, or deploying it through a tag management system such as Google Tag Manager, Tealium, or Adobe so it fires site-wide from one container. A tag added to a single landing page rather than the global template leaves every other page untracked.

When the tag is missing or only partly deployed, the loss is not limited to one report. The same tag feeds conversions, audiences, and demographics, so a gap removes all three at once on the affected pages. A campaign optimizing toward website conversions then learns from data that is incomplete or empty.

A few signs point to the tag being absent or firing on only part of the site:
  • Campaign Manager shows the Insight Tag status as inactive, or it never moved past the initial unverified state
  • The tag inspector finds the tag on the home page but not on conversion or confirmation pages
  • A tag manager container holds the tag but the trigger is scoped to specific pages rather than all pages
You may want to confirm the tag both exists and fires on a sample of pages across the site, not only the home page, before deciding it is installed. Campaign Manager reports the tag as active once it sees a member visit, which can take up to 24 hours after placement.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a considered-purchase or B2B store, the Insight Tag still belongs in the global template so it covers product, cart, and order confirmation pages alike. The order confirmation page is where a purchase conversion would be recorded, so you may want to confirm it fires the tag in particular.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, which is the common LinkedIn case, the Insight Tag should fire on the form page and the thank-you or confirmation page that follows a submission. The confirmation page is usually where the lead conversion is counted, so you may want to confirm both pages load the tag.

learn_more.source_title: Add the LinkedIn Insight Tag to your website
Add the LinkedIn site-wide Insight Tag to Google Tag Manager

investigate.intro: Confirm the Insight Tag exists in Campaign Manager, reads as active, and fires on a sample of pages across the site rather than the home page alone.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: In Campaign Manager, open the 'Analyze' menu and select 'Insight Tag'.

Note the tag's status. An active status means LinkedIn has recorded at least one member visit. A status still showing as unverified after 24 hours of live traffic points to a tag that is not firing where members land.
Note the Partner ID shown for the tag. You will use it to confirm the right tag is on the pages.

tag_inspector.label: Browser tag inspector
steps: Open a browser extension that lists network requests or fired tags, such as a tag assistant, then load the home page.
Look for a request to LinkedIn's tracking endpoint and confirm it carries the account's Partner ID.
Repeat on a conversion or confirmation page and any secondary section of the site. A tag that fires on the home page but not on these pages is deployed too narrowly to cover conversions.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per sampled page, whether the Insight Tag fired and whether it carried the account Partner ID. A site where the tag fires on every sampled page, including conversion and confirmation pages, with the correct Partner ID passes. A site where the tag is missing in Campaign Manager, reads as inactive, or fails to fire on one or more sampled pages is a fail recorded with the pages that came back untagged.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the Insight Tag status from Campaign Manager and the fired-or-not observation for each sampled page above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the LinkedIn Insight Tag is not firing across {{site_name}}. The Insight Tag is the source of LinkedIn's website measurement, so where it is absent the website conversions go uncounted, retargeting audiences cannot build, and website demographic reporting has no data, and any campaign optimizing toward conversions learns from an incomplete picture. {{voice_recommend}} placing the Insight Tag in the site's global footer or deploying it site-wide through the tag management system, so it fires on every page including conversion and confirmation pages. After the change, confirm in Campaign Manager that the tag reads as active and verify with a tag inspector that it fires with the correct Partner ID on a sample of pages.

audit_checks.title: Multiple Insight Tags or a Partner ID mismatch is firing on the site
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When two Insight Tags fire on the same page, page views and conversions are counted twice and retargeting audiences inflate with duplicate members. A wrong Partner ID sends the data to a different tag than the one the campaigns read, so the reporting account shows fewer conversions than actually happened.

educate.base:
Each Insight Tag carries a Partner ID that ties it to one ad account. The intended setup is a single Insight Tag, with the account's Partner ID, firing once per page. Two situations break that. The first is a duplicate tag, where the Insight Tag is hardcoded in the site template and a second copy is also deployed through a tag manager, so both fire on every page. The second is a Partner ID mismatch, where the tag on the site carries a different Partner ID than the account running the campaigns.

A duplicate tag double-counts. Page views register twice, conversions can fire twice, and website retargeting audiences fill with duplicate entries, so audience sizes read larger than the real visitor count. A Partner ID mismatch is the opposite problem. The data lands in whichever account owns the Partner ID on the page, so the account the auditor is reviewing shows fewer conversions and a smaller audience than the traffic should produce.

Both trace back to how the tag was deployed over time. A site that switched from a hardcoded tag to a tag manager without removing the original, or an account restructure where the Partner ID changed, can leave the wrong combination live.

You may want to load a page and count how many LinkedIn tracking requests fire, then read the Partner ID on each. Exactly one request, carrying the account's own Partner ID, is the target. More than one, or one with a Partner ID that does not match the account, points to a deployment to clean up.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a considered-purchase or B2B store, a duplicate tag on the order confirmation page double-counts purchases, which makes the LinkedIn-reported conversion volume read higher than the orders the store actually received. You may want to inspect the confirmation page specifically for a second LinkedIn request.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, a duplicate tag on the thank-you page double-counts leads, and a Partner ID mismatch routes those leads to the wrong account. You may want to confirm the form and thank-you pages each fire one LinkedIn request with the account's Partner ID.

learn_more.source_title: Create and manage your Insight Tag

investigate.intro: Confirm the account's Partner ID in Campaign Manager, then check that exactly one Insight Tag with that Partner ID fires per page.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: In Campaign Manager, open 'Analyze', then 'Insight Tag', and note the Partner ID for the account you are auditing.
If the account uses more than one Insight Tag, note each Partner ID so you can tell an intended second tag apart from an unintended duplicate.

tag_inspector.label: Browser tag inspector
steps: Load a page that should fire the tag, then list the network requests sent to LinkedIn's tracking endpoint.
Count the requests. More than one Insight Tag request on a single page indicates a duplicate.
Read the Partner ID on each request and compare it to the Partner ID from Campaign Manager. A request whose Partner ID does not match the account is sending data to the wrong tag.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per sampled page, the number of LinkedIn Insight Tag requests that fired and the Partner ID on each. A page that fires exactly one request carrying the account's Partner ID passes. A page that fires more than one request, or a request whose Partner ID does not match the account, is a fail recorded with the page and the Partner IDs observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the count of Insight Tag requests and the Partner ID on each for the sampled pages above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} is firing more than one Insight Tag, or an Insight Tag with a Partner ID that does not match the account. A duplicate tag double-counts page views and conversions and inflates retargeting audiences with duplicate members, while a Partner ID mismatch routes the data to a different account, so the reviewed account undercounts conversions. {{voice_recommend}} removing the duplicate so exactly one Insight Tag fires per page, and confirming the remaining tag carries the account's own Partner ID. After the change, reload a sample of pages and verify a single LinkedIn request fires on each with the correct Partner ID.

audit_checks.title: Site domains are not added to the Insight Tag allow-list
audit_checks.impact_rationale: LinkedIn records website activity only for domains it associates with the Insight Tag. Pages on a subdomain or a secondary domain that is not associated go untracked even when the tag is physically present, so conversions and audiences on those domains never register in the account.

educate.base:
An Insight Tag is associated with the domains it sees traffic on. LinkedIn populates this domain list automatically once the tag fires and a member visits, and Campaign Manager lets an advertiser review the list and block any domain it does not recognize. Conversion tracking, website retargeting audiences, and website professional demographics are all scoped to the associated domains.

The gap shows up when a campaign drives traffic to a domain or subdomain that is not associated with the tag. A site might run its main pages on one domain and host a campaign landing page, a checkout flow, or a regional store on a separate subdomain or a different domain entirely. If the tag is present there but the domain has not been associated, or has been blocked, LinkedIn does not record activity on it. The conversions and audiences for that traffic never reach the account.

Because LinkedIn populates the list from observed traffic, a newly launched domain can take a short time to appear after the tag first fires there. A domain that never appears after traffic has run for a day points to a tag that is not firing on it or a domain that has been blocked rather than allowed.

You may want to list every domain and subdomain the campaigns send traffic to, then confirm each one is associated with the Insight Tag in Campaign Manager and is not blocked. A landing page that converts well but sits on an unassociated domain reports nothing back.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a considered-purchase or B2B store, the checkout or order confirmation step sometimes runs on a separate payment or platform subdomain. An unassociated checkout domain records no purchase conversions, so you may want to confirm that subdomain is associated with the Insight Tag.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, campaign landing pages and the form's thank-you page sometimes live on a marketing subdomain that differs from the main site. You may want to confirm every domain the campaigns route to, including those landing and thank-you pages, is associated with the tag.

learn_more.source_title: Set up Conversion Tracking for Insight Tag conversions in Campaign Manager

investigate.intro: Confirm that every domain and subdomain the campaigns drive traffic to is associated with the Insight Tag in Campaign Manager and is not blocked.

linkedin_campaign_manager.label: LinkedIn Campaign Manager
steps: In Campaign Manager, open 'Analyze', then 'Insight Tag', and review the list of domains associated with the tag.
Compare that list against every domain and subdomain the campaigns route traffic to, including landing pages, checkout flows, and regional or secondary sites.
Note any domain receiving campaign traffic that is missing from the list or marked as blocked. Blocking a domain removes its conversions, audiences, and demographics.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner lists every domain and subdomain the campaigns drive traffic to, then records for each whether it is associated with the Insight Tag and not blocked. An account where every traffic-receiving domain is associated and unblocked passes. An account with a traffic-receiving domain missing from the associated list or marked as blocked is a fail recorded with the domains that are not covered.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each campaign domain and whether it appears as associated and unblocked in the Insight Tag domain list above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more domains receiving campaign traffic for {{site_name}} are not associated with the Insight Tag. LinkedIn records website activity only for associated domains, so conversions and retargeting audiences on an unassociated subdomain or secondary domain never reach the account even when the tag is present on those pages. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the Insight Tag domain list in Campaign Manager against every domain and subdomain the campaigns route to, and confirming each is associated and not blocked. After the change, allow a day of traffic and recheck that the previously missing domains appear in the associated list.
